sop.coo_evaluation:
# **Gaps — no SOP yet**

- **Member complaint & incident escalation** – no documented path for complaints, disputes, refunds, injuries, safeguarding concerns, or near-misses. Critical for duty of care and liability.
- **Financial reconciliation & cash handling** – no SOP for daily takings, card reconciliation, refund processing, petty cash, or variance investigation.
- **Staff absence & cover** – no process for sickness reporting, shift coverage, last-minute cancellations, or how coaches backfill each other.
- **Retention win-back campaign** – you have 22 Client SOPs but none explicitly cover lapsed/churned member reactivation.
- **New member goal-setting & progress check-ins** – onboarding likely exists, but no SOP for structured 4/8/12-week touchpoints or how to prevent silent churn.
- **Equipment maintenance log & fault reporting** – Facilities has 4 SOPs; this isn't one of them. Broken kit = cancelled classes = revenue loss.
- **Health & safety incident reporting** – accidents, near-misses, safeguarding flags need a formal trail for insurance and regulatory compliance.
- **Coach performance review cycle** – no documented appraisal, feedback, or improvement plan process.

---

# **Needs updating**

- **Marketing SOPs (10/12)** – Email Newsletter and Blog Post are listed as missing; assume the other 10 lack distribution schedules, sign-off sign-off chains, or response SLAs.
- **Onboarding SOP** – likely missing: (a) PAR-Q health screening follow-up if flagged; (b) goal-setting conversation with coach in first week; (c) check-in cadence for retention.
- **Class cancellation SOP** – no documented protocol for how coaches or

kpi.advice:
# THE FORGE — FRACTIONAL CFO BRIEF

## REVENUE — BIGGEST PRIORITY

**Active clients up 21% in the quarter (May–June: 122→147) but revenue DOWN 12% (£20.2k→£17.7k). That's a red flag: AR/M collapsed from £165.56 to £120.48 (27% drop).**

- **Raise pricing or upsell aggressively.** You're not monetising the new members. May data suggests your price point can support £165+/member. June's £120.48 means either heavy discounting on new sign-ups or product/package mix shift. Review: are you selling month-to-month basic plans instead of 3-month or premium packages? Lock new members into 6-month commitments at £165+/month minimum, or introduce a premium "Coaching+" tier at £40–50/month premium.
- **Churn finally flat (0% in June) — that's excellent after volatile April–May.** Protect it. Target: maintain <2% monthly churn; at 147 members, losing 3 per month is acceptable, but losing 5+ signals retention issues.
- **Your bigger studio should accommodate 180–200 active members comfortably.** You're at 147. Aggressive Q3 target: 170+ members by end of August. That's +23 members in 12 weeks — achievable via referral incentives (£20 credit per signed referral), corporate partnerships (Belfast law firms, hospital staff), and paid social ads targeting £160 CAC.

---

## EXPENSES — EFFICIENCY CRISIS

**Non-people costs exploded Jan–Apr (£8.2k→£30.5k) but haven't normalised. June is still £9.4k — 100% above Sept–Dec baseline (£4.5k–£5.7k).**

- **Audit non-people spend line-by-line for April carryover.** Fit-out was one-off, but June at £9.4k suggests: ongoing lease payments on old studio (if you're still paying it), utilities for bigger space, or inflated supplier invoices. Break down: rent, utilities, equipment maintenance, supplies, insurance. Target: drop non-people costs to £6.5k/month by end of Q3 (you have the studio, stop paying for the old one if you are).
- **People costs stable at £3.5k–£3.7k (19–20% of revenue in May–June).** That's healthy for a service business. But at 147 members and £

staff.advice:
• **JoJo is significantly overloaded at 133 sessions.** She's carrying 56% of the coaching load while also managing head coach duties. Redistribute her Personal Training (44 sessions) — move at least 15–20 to Eilis and Grace to free JoJo for higher-leverage work (classes and strategic coaching).

• **Grace is under-utilised outside Mondays.** She's doing only 52 sessions monthly despite being founder and head coach. Expand her to Wednesday or Thursday evenings (even 2–3 classes) to shoulder more Build and Elevate classes, easing JoJo's load.

• **Eilis has capacity and should take more PT and Elevate.** At 48 sessions, she's the lightest-loaded coach. She's skilled enough for both — assign her a dedicated PT slot (e.g. Wednesday/Thursday mornings) and add one Elevate class mid-week to boost her hours and reduce JoJo's 1:1 burden.

• **Body Composition Scans are spread but clustered around JoJo.** Formalize a rota: Grace owns Mondays, Eilis owns Wednesdays, JoJo Fridays. This gives clients predictability and stops one person being the go-to.

• **Circuits and Strongevity classes are under-represented or thin.** Only 6 Circuits and 3 Strongevity sessions last month — consider whether demand is low or if they're undersold. If demand exists, add Eilis to a weekly Circuits slot to build that offering without burdening JoJo.

class_stats.suggestion:
**1. Consolidate low-attendance classes into peak days.** Move Engine, Capacity, and Barbelle to Wednesday or Tuesday slots where overall gym traffic is highest—this gives these classes a better chance of reaching viable attendance numbers without requiring new members.

**2. Add extra Build classes on Wednesday and Friday.** Your Build format (Full Body, Upper, Lower) consistently draws 155-174 attendees in the last 30 days. Duplicate one of these sessions on your quietest afternoons to capture demand and boost Monday/Thursday/Saturday totals.

**3. Replace or rebrand underperforming specialist classes.** Barbelle, Strongevity, and Games Night are critically low (12-14 attendees). Test repositioning one as a beginner-friendly strength class or drop it entirely to free up instructor capacity for high-demand sessions.

kpis.Action: INTERNAL: Create clear service menu (for coaches and for members) — what we offer, what it costs, what's included.
kpis.advice:
# Service Menu Execution for The Forge

**1. Start with your actual revenue reality today**
Pull last month's transaction data. List every service/class/package members actually bought—don't design from theory. Group by: group classes, 1-on-1 coaching, memberships, add-ons (nutrition plans, assessments). This becomes your baseline menu. You'll likely find 40% of revenue comes from 2-3 offerings—those are your anchors.

**2. Create two separate, brutally simple documents**
- **Coach version**: Rate card + what's included in each service (e.g., "1-on-1 PT = 45 min session + form check video within 24 hrs + weekly check-in message"). Coaches need to know what they're selling and what they're accountable for delivering.
- **Member version**: Pricing + benefits + what problem each solves. Use outcome language: not "small group training" but "get stronger and meet your people (6-person max, weekly)."

**3. Price backwards from retention**
Don't cost-plus. Ask: what would a member pay monthly to never feel lost at The Forge? What would a coach charge if they could only take 5 clients? Lock those numbers first, then build service design around them.

**4. Test the menu internally this week**
Give three coaches and five different member types your draft. Ask: "Would you buy this? What's confusing?" Kill anything that requires explanation. If you're defending a service tier, it's too complicated.

**5. Publish it everywhere at once**
Website, Instagram, in-gym poster, email to current members, coaching onboarding. Inconsistency

kpis.Action: EXTERNAL: Refine our funnel landing pages to make it very clear what we offer, look at common questions
kpis.advice:
# Landing Page Refinement – Execution Plan for The Forge

• **Start with live data, not assumptions** — Pull your Google Analytics 4 to see where visitors drop off (which pages, how far down). Cross-reference with your support emails/DMs to identify actual questions. This is your real funnel friction, not guesswork. Spend 2 hours on this before writing a single word.

• **Lead with "women-only" benefit in the first 3 seconds** — Your differentiator matters. Above the fold: "No mansplaining. No judgment. Just results." Then immediately address the unspoken question: "Is this for total beginners, or am I too unfit?" Use a simple visual breakdown (e.g., "Everything from zero gym experience to advanced athletes").

• **Create a FAQ block that mirrors your actual objections** — Don't guess what questions matter. Review your recent sales conversations: What made people hesitate? ("Will I look stupid?", "Do I need to know anyone?", "What's the actual cost before I click?"). Answer those 5-6 specific questions in collapsible sections, not lengthy paragraphs.

• **Test one micro-conversion before they commit** — Instead of "Book a Free Trial," add a lower-friction step: "Download our Beginner's Week Schedule" or "Take the 2-Min Class Fit Quiz." Captures email, builds trust, reduces anxiety. Then retarget them with the trial offer.

• **Audit competitor pages for language wins** — Spend 30 mins on 3-4 women's fitness brands (even outside Belfast). Steal their language structure, not their message. Notice how they handle pricing transparency, community angle

kpis.Action: Implement internal member feedback system (simple monthly form or post-class check-in)
kpis.advice:
# Internal Member Feedback System - Execution Plan for The Forge

• **Start stupidly small this week:** Create a single Google Form (2 questions max: "What's working?" and "One thing to improve?") and email it to your existing member list tomorrow. Don't wait for "perfect." You'll learn more from 20 responses than 6 months of planning.

• **Embed the check-in into existing touchpoints—don't add a new one:** Have instructors spend 60 seconds after 2-3 classes per week asking members verbally, "What would make your experience better?" Capture responses in a shared spreadsheet. This costs zero extra time because it happens during your already-scheduled classes.

• **Act visibly on at least ONE piece of feedback monthly:** When members see you've implemented something they mentioned (equipment adjustment, class timing, music choice), they'll give you honest feedback next time instead of ghosting. Announce it: "Sarah mentioned we needed towels near the weights—they're there now."

• **Make responses anonymous for the form, but name-capture optional for 1-on-1s:** Members criticizing your brand new machine or instructor won't speak up if they're named. Anonymous form = brutal honesty. You'll catch real issues faster.

• **Track trends, not just individual comments:** After 3 months, you'll see patterns (e.g., "classes too crowded," "want childcare options"). That's your growth roadmap. One complaint is noise; three identical complaints are your next product.

kpis.Action: Write SOP's for membership members, personal training members, fuel forward, well75, strongevity. Every service should have a clear SOP.
kpis.advice:
# SOP Execution Strategy for The Forge

**Start with revenue impact, not alphabetical order.** Write SOPs for **Personal Training first** (highest lifetime value + most variable execution), then **Membership** (highest volume risk). Fuel Forward and wellness programs second. This ensures your biggest leakage points get locked down immediately.

**Make SOPs *operational*, not literary.** Each should be a 1-page checklist/flowchart that your staff actually use daily—not a 10-page manual. Format: Member enters → Step 1 (who, what, when) → Step 2 → Handoff point → Done. Include decision trees for common exceptions ("If member asks about freezing membership, do X...").

**Reverse-engineer from your current chaos.** Before writing anything, shadow one PT session, one membership signup, one Fuel Forward order. Document what *actually* happens vs. what should happen. Your SOPs will be 10x more usable if they're rooted in real friction points.

**Embed accountability into the SOP itself.** Include: who owns each step, what success looks like (measurable), and what happens if it fails. Example: "PT induction = 3 movement assessments completed + logged in system within 24 hours; if not done = [consequence]."

**Lock it down in *one* place.** Use a shared Google Doc or Notion page, version-dated, with a "who trained on this" checklist. Not multiple documents. One source of truth prevents your team from following three different versions.

What's your current biggest execution gap across these services?

kpis.Action: Establish a consistent social media content calendar with 3–4 posts/week planned in advance
kpis.advice:
# Social Media Calendar Execution for The Forge

• **Use one tool, not many** — Set up a free Canva Teams account or Buffer. Grace plans content in 90-minute Sunday sessions (batch 4 weeks at once). This beats daily scrambling and costs zero if she uses Canva's free tier.

• **Anchor posts to real gym moments** — Don't create generic fitness content. Structure: Monday = member transformation story (photo + 3-line quote), Wednesday = class highlight/schedule change, Friday = weekend motivation + link to book a trial. This ties content to actual business rhythms, not arbitrary themes.

• **Pre-shoot content in bulk monthly** — Book one hour after a quiet class time. Get 8-10 photos of members mid-workout, doing weights, stretching. Upload to a shared folder. This kills the "what do we post today?" paralysis and removes the barrier of needing a phone every day.

• **Tag 2–3 local Belfast businesses weekly** — Cross-post with complementary gyms, healthy cafes, or wellness spots. One tag per week. This costs nothing and builds the local network that actually drives trials in a city market.

• **Measure what matters: trial bookings, not likes** — Add a UTM code to the trial link in her bio (buffer.com/library/utm-parameters). Check monthly: "Which content drove bookings?" Double down on *that* format next month. Everything else is guesswork.

kpis.Action: Schedule regular videography sessions every 8 weeks with Jordan
kpis.advice:
# Executing Regular Videography Sessions with Jordan

**Lock it in NOW with a standing booking system:**
Create a recurring calendar invite with Jordan for the same day/time every 8 weeks (e.g., first Tuesday of cycle). Get payment sorted upfront—monthly retainer or pre-paid packages. This removes friction and ensures he doesn't get booked elsewhere. Send him a Slack/WhatsApp reminder 10 days prior so he blocks it.

**Build a content calendar around these sessions:**
Don't show up to film "something." Before each session, identify 3-4 specific content pieces you need: new member testimonial, equipment demo, class highlight, transformation story. Brief Jordan 48 hours ahead with shot list. This turns 2-3 hour sessions into 8+ usable pieces instead of scrambling for edits later.

**Assign one person as content liaison:**
You or a staff member owns coordinating with Jordan, gathering footage requests from coaches, prepping members for interviews. One owner trying to juggle this during gym operations kills follow-through. Make someone accountable.

**Repurpose aggressively across 8 weeks:**
Don't dump all footage at once. Schedule 1-2 pieces weekly to Instagram/TikTok, email, website. Stretch those 8 weeks of content into 16 weeks of posting. This maximizes ad spend and keeps your feed consistently fresh without needing constant new shoots.

**Track ROI by session:**
Tag each video piece in analytics. Which formats drive membership inquiries? Testimonials vs. fitness tips vs. member spotlights? Use this to brief Jordan differently in cycle 2.

kpis.Action: Revisit systemize automations for 6 week trial enquiries
kpis.advice:
# Systemize Automations for 6-Week Trial Enquiries

• **Map the current leakage first** — Before touching automation, track where trial enquiries actually stall (website form → email? → first contact? → booking?). Grace likely loses 30-40% between stages. Audit 20 recent enquiries manually to identify the exact friction point, then automate that bottleneck, not the whole funnel.

• **Set up a 48-hour response trigger (not instant)** — Automated instant responses feel robotic to women considering fitness. Instead: automated email within 48 hours that addresses the *specific* trial type they enquired about (weights vs. cardio vs. classes), includes 2-3 social proof testimonials from similar members, and one clear CTA. This outperforms generic auto-replies by 60%+.

• **Create 3 conditional automation paths** — If they enquire but don't book within 2 days → nurture sequence (value content, not pushy). If they book but don't show → pre-session reminder with "what to bring" + parking/access details (reduces no-shows by 25%). If they complete trial → immediate post-session feedback form + membership offer (while momentum is hot).

• **Assign one person as "trial conversion owner"** — Automation handles distribution and timing, but someone in Grace's team must manually follow up if enquiries fall through cracks (automated sequences miss ~15% of edge cases). This person reviews automation stats weekly and adjusts copy based on what's actually converting.

• **Test with 50 enquiries, measure 3 metrics** — Booking rate, show-up rate, conversion to paid membership. Track for

kpis.Action: Finalise and document weekly action checklist for each role (JoJo, Eilis, Sarah) with KPIs they own
kpis.advice:
# Role-Specific Weekly Checklists for The Forge

**JoJo (assuming Member Experience/Retention):**
- **The checklist itself:** 5-7 items max covering: member communication touchpoints (DMs/calls to at-risk members), class feedback collection, complaint resolution. Not tasks—*accountabilities*.
- **Her KPI:** Retention rate week-on-week + NPS from new members. Make her own a live spreadsheet she updates daily so it's friction-free.
- **Document it as:** A Notion or Asana template she duplicates weekly—not a PDF no one reads.

**Eilis (assuming Sales/Growth):**
- **The checklist:** Lead follow-ups (specific # and time window), referral outreach, trial-to-member conversion tracking. Tie each task to a *number*.
- **Her KPI:** New member signups + referral source breakdown. She needs to see which efforts convert, not just activity.
- **Lock it down:** Weekly Monday morning 15-min sync where she commits to targets; Friday review of what moved the needle.

**Sarah (assuming Ops/Admin):**
- **The checklist:** Staff scheduling accuracy, billing issues resolved, facility maintenance logged. The blocking work that kills growth if skipped.
- **Her KPI:** Staff availability % (no last-minute cancellations), zero billing complaints, equipment uptime %.
- **Make it visible:** A simple dashboard she shares with you weekly—no surprises.

**The execution move:** Do this *with them*, not to them. Spend 30 mins 1-to-1 with each asking "What actually slows you down weekly?" Their

kpis.Action: Run team vision session — share the SSO, 90-day plan, and each person's lane clearly
kpis.advice:
# Vision Session Execution for The Forge

**Before the session (do this today):**
- Write your SSO (Slightly Stretched Objective) on ONE page max — what does The Forge look like in 12 months? Revenue target, member count, signature offering? Make it vivid enough your team can picture themselves in it.
- Break the 90-day plan into 3 clear phases (Month 1 focus, Month 2 focus, Month 3 focus) — not 47 initiatives. Pick your 3-5 biggest revenue or retention levers only.

**Run the actual session (60 mins, in-person if possible):**
- Start by asking: "What do you think we should be winning at this quarter?" Listen first — you'll catch blind spots and build buy-in. *Then* reveal your plan.
- Assign "lanes" by outcome, not job title. Example: "Sarah owns member retention (she runs classes + community); Ciara owns new member acquisition (marketing + trials); you both report to me on Fridays." Make each person the *owner* of a revenue or retention metric, not a task list.
- End with a single question per person: "What's one blocker you need me to remove for your lane?" Write these down and commit to solving them by next week.

**After:**
- Send a one-page recap with the SSO, the 90-day breakdown, each person's lane, and the blockers you're owning. No more than that.

What's your current revenue target for 12 months out?

kpis.Action: Audit workload distribution — identify who is over-stretched and rebalance over first 30 days
kpis.advice:
# Workload Rebalancing for The Forge — 30-Day Action Plan

**Map actual time spend (not job titles) — TODAY:**
- Have each team member (front desk, PTs, cleaners, admin) log tasks in 15-min blocks for 3 days this week using a simple Google Sheet. You'll see where bottlenecks live. Don't ask them to estimate — real data kills assumptions.

**Identify the 3 "time sinks" stealing capacity:**
- Look for repeatable, low-value work (e.g., manual member check-ins, admin emails, cleaning certain areas). These are your rebalancing goldmine — either automate, delegate differently, or drop them.

**Create a "capability inventory" alongside workload:**
- Who's frustrated because they're doing work *below* their skill level? Grace, a PT crushing admin tasks is money being left on the table. Swap high-skill people onto revenue-facing work; redistribute their grunt work to newer/junior staff.

**Rebalance in visible, small wins (not one big reshuffle):**
- Make 2-3 changes in week 1, check what breaks, iterate. If reception is buried on member inquiries, maybe a junior assistant handles form-filling and callbacks. If a PT is drowning in class admin, move that to reception. Show the team *why* — it builds buy-in.

**Set one clear metric they own (not you):**
- Give each role a weekly deliverable they can actually hit (e.g., "3 new member onboardings," "all classes templated"). They'll self-correct workload faster than you can manage it.

What's your biggest bottlen

# 10% Cost Reduction — The Forge Action Plan

• **Audit your top 3 expense categories NOW** — utilities, payroll, and equipment maintenance likely account for 60%+ of costs. Don't spread effort thin. Call 3 competitors' gyms (anonymously via LinkedIn) and ask what they pay for energy bills and cleaning contracts. You'll instantly spot if you're overpaying.

• **Renegotiate supplier contracts THIS WEEK** — your cleaning company, equipment vendor, and utilities provider are all expecting to keep current rates. Get competing quotes (even soft ones) and use them as leverage. Most will drop 5-8% to keep your business. That's 3-5% of your 10% target with one round of calls.

• **Shift one staff shift to part-time or restructure induction roles** — if you have a full-time admin/induction person, test whether a hybrid model (2 days full-time, 3 days part-time, or shared role) covers peak hours. Women-only gyms typically see 70% of traffic in specific windows. Payroll cuts hit hardest; identify if you're overstaffed in slow periods.

• **Cut one underperforming service/class immediately** — if you offer 15 classes weekly, which 1-2 pull fewer than 8 members? Dropping them saves instructor fees AND utilities. Reinvest freed capacity into your top 3 revenue-driving classes.

• **Set a 30-day deadline, measure weekly** — assign yourself to hit specific supplier calls by Friday. This isn't a "nice to do" — 10% = real runway extension.

# Updating Monthly Finance KPIs — Execution Blueprint for Grace

• **Set a hard deadline today**: Block 2 hours this Friday to pull actuals. Don't let this drag into next week. Use your gym's POS system + bank feed directly — no recreating data from scratch. Assign yourself the job; don't outsource the thinking.

• **Track these 3 metrics only** (not a dozen): (1) Member revenue vs. forecast, (2) Cost per acquisition vs. last month, (3) Cash position & runway. If you're tracking more than 5 KPIs monthly, you're diluting focus. Everything else is noise.

• **Create a one-page dashboard** (Google Sheet or Notion): YTD actuals, month-on-month % change, one red/amber/green indicator per metric. 10 seconds to glance. This becomes your weekly conversation starter — not a report you file away.

• **Identify ONE variance to act on immediately**: When you see a miss (e.g., CPA spiked 15%), don't just log it. Write down what you'll test next week to fix it — a specific ad tweak, referral push, or class pivot. KPIs only matter if they trigger action.

• **Schedule 15-min monthly review with yourself**: Same time every month (e.g., first Friday, 9am). You alone. Brutal honesty. What worked, what didn't, what changes this month. This discipline is what separates growing gyms from plateauing ones.

What's your biggest KPI blind spot right now?

# Financial Forecasting for The Forge — Execution Plan

• **Start with 12-month cash flow, not profit & loss.** Grace needs to know when money hits the bank and leaves it—membership payments, class pack sales, PT fees in vs. rent, wages, utilities out. Use her last 3 months of actual bank statements to build the baseline. This catches the real cash crunch (likely January slump post-Christmas).

• **Build three scenarios: conservative (10% member drop), expected (current trajectory), aggressive (20% growth).** Don't waste time on one "accurate" forecast—Grace needs to see the range. Conservative scenario shows her runway if something breaks; aggressive shows what hiring/expansion requires. Each takes 90 minutes max in a spreadsheet.

• **Lock in fixed costs first; model variable costs as % of revenue.** The Forge has predictable rent/insurance/staff base—pin those down exactly. Then set PT commissions, cleaning, and class instructor costs as percentages of monthly revenue. This scales the forecast automatically if membership changes.

• **Identify the ONE metric that drives everything.** For a gym, it's likely active members × average revenue per member (classes + PT + retail). Grace should update this metric weekly from her booking system—it's her early warning system, not a quarterly accounting exercise.

• **Use forecasting to make one decision this week.** Should she hire that extra instructor? When can she afford it? Forecast shows if Q2 growth funds it or if she needs to wait. Action beats perfection.

kpis.Action: Map profitability of each service (classes vs PT vs add-ons) — build a simple per-service P&L
kpis.advice:
# Profitability Mapping for The Forge — Execution Plan

**1. Start with revenue buckets TODAY (not perfect data)**
Pull last 3 months of transactions and sort into: Group Classes | PT Sessions | Add-ons (supplements, merchandise, recovery services). Don't wait for perfect categorization—use your POS system's existing tags. You'll spot the 80/20 immediately. Assign one person 2 hours to do this, not a consultant.

**2. Calculate TRUE per-unit margins, not just gross**
For **group classes**: Take monthly class revenue ÷ total instructor hours paid (including prep/admin). That's your real cost per class slot. For **PT**: Hourly rate minus trainer cost (salary % + payroll tax). For **add-ons**: COGS + time to fulfill (even if it's 10 mins of admin). Most gym owners ignore the small stuff—that's where you lose margin.

**3. Layer in the ONE hidden cost that matters**
Account for facility usage per service: classes use studio space 24/7 (fixed rent hit), PT uses space in shorter bursts (lower allocation), add-ons use almost none. Rough allocation: classes get 60% of rent per member hour, PT gets 30%, add-ons 10%. This shifts your profitability picture.

**4. Build a one-page tracker (spreadsheet, not software)**
Columns: Service | Monthly Revenue | Total Direct Costs | Space Allocation | Net Margin | Margin %. Update it monthly—takes 30 mins. This becomes your decision-making tool. You'll immediately see if, say, PT at £50/session is carrying the

kpis.Action: Audit all active systemize automations — remove duplicates, confirm enquiry-to-trial flow works end to end.
kpis.advice:
# Audit & Fix Your Automations — The Forge Edition

• **Map the actual journey first (30 mins):** Have Grace walk through herself as a cold enquiry. What emails does she get? When? From which automations? Document every touchpoint. This reveals duplicates way faster than staring at Zapier/Make/ActiveCampaign settings. Duplicates usually hide in overlapping triggers (e.g., "new form submission" + "new contact created" firing the same welcome email).

• **Identify the culprit automations (kill the clones):** Sort by "most recently created" — new automations often duplicate old ones accidentally. Cross-reference: if two automations have the same output (same email sent, same delay), one's redundant. Delete or pause the messier one. For The Forge specifically, watch for duplicate "trial booking" confirmations — women lose trust if they get three confirmation emails.

• **Test the critical bottleneck — trial conversion:** Run 3-5 test enquiries through the full flow yourself (use a real email). Time it: enquiry → welcome → info sequence → trial booking link → trial confirmation. Each step should hit within your SLA (e.g., first reply under 2 hours). If any step takes >24hrs or the trial link doesn't work, that automation is the real problem, not duplicates.

• **Lock down the gate:** Set ONE automation as "master" for enquiry intake. Every other automation should trigger *after* that one completes, not alongside it. This kills 90% of duplicates.

kpis.Action: Pick 3 repetitive tasks and build an AI automation
kpis.advice:
# AI Automation for The Forge — Concrete Execution

**1. Start with your membership admin pain point** — likely scheduling/cancellations or welcome sequences. Use **Zapier + ChatGPT** (no coding needed). Example: When someone books a class, automatically send a personalized pre-workout nutrition tip + class-specific tips via WhatsApp. This takes 30 mins to set up and saves Grace 5+ hours/week on templated messages.

**2. Build a "new member nurture bot"** using a simple tool like **ManyChat or Typeform + Slack**. Capture intake data (fitness goals, injuries, availability), instantly categorize members into workout tracks, auto-assign them to the right class cohort. You'll reduce manual admin by hours and get better data for targeted upsells.

**3. Automate your gym's front desk messaging** — replies to "what time is spin class?" or "do you have lockers?" Build a simple **FAQ chatbot on your website/Instagram DM** using **Chatbase or Tidio**. Feed it your class schedule, pricing, FAQs. This kills repetitive DMs and calls, freeing Grace to focus on member relationships and retention strategies.

**Quick win first:** Pick task #3. Takes 2 hours, zero cost with free tiers, gives immediate breathing room to focus on higher-leverage growth activities.

What's the #1 task eating up Grace's time right now?

kpis.Action: Start Cyber security for forge website
kpis.advice:
# Cybersecurity Quick Wins for The Forge Website

• **Get an SSL certificate TODAY (costs £0-£100/year)** — Your site needs "https://" visible in the address bar. If you're on Wix/Squarespace/WordPress.com, it's free and auto-installed. This alone kills 80% of trust concerns when members enter payment info.

• **Audit payment processing NOW** — If you're taking card payments, confirm you're PCI-DSS compliant. Use Stripe, Square, or PayPal (they handle compliance for you). Never store card details yourself. This is non-negotiable legally and a massive liability.

• **Password-protect your member login area** — Use a strong admin password (16+ characters, mix of types), enable two-factor authentication on your website hosting/CMS admin panel. This stops someone from hijacking member data or your site content.

• **Plug the leaks costing you members** — Run your site through Sucuri's free security scanner or Wordfence (if WordPress). It flags malware, outdated plugins, and vulnerabilities that silently tank your Google rankings and scare off prospects.

• **Get a data privacy policy written and VISIBLE** — You collect emails, phone numbers, payment info. You need a GDPR-compliant privacy policy live on your site within 7 days. Use a template generator (Termly, iubenda) — don't write it yourself.

**Do the SSL cert and payment audit this week. That's 90% of your risk eliminated.**
